courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Learning GPA
courses.description: This is a basic orientation course for Government and Public Administration (GPA) students. It will familiarize students with the essence of politics and the study of it. Students will be introduced to the basic approaches, methods of study, research and writing skills, and ways of thinking of the discipline.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge Outcome: Understanding of basic approaches and methods of studying politics.

Skills Outcome: Mastery of essential analytical and communication skills for studying politics, particularly the abilities to engage in independent and critical thinking.

Value Outcome: Awareness of the importance of politics in daily life and studies; appreciation and tolerance of different perspectives in understanding politics and interpret political phenomena; and a sense of purpose, civic responsibility, global citizenship, and commitment in life.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Understanding the essence of politics
2. Basic approaches of politics
3. Method of studying politics
4. Essay research and writing skills in politics
5. Way of thinking of politics
courses.feedback_evaluation: Course evaluation; focus group; teacher's self reflection; informal peer review.

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Political Debate
courses.description: This course will study political debates both from theoretical as well as practical perspectives. It aims at developing students’ confidence in presenting political arguments in public, and to defend the ideas in face of criticisms. Training will also be offered in respect of the skills in delivering sophisticated political messages in a coherent and convincing manner, and the professional way to restate a refined position. The course will also cover certain methodology of investigation and formal analysis of political activities and dialogues.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken UGEC2711.

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Politics and Mass Media
courses.description: This course tries to explore the theoretical and practical links between politics and the media. It will examine the interplay of the media, the general public and the governing process in modern liberal democracy, and see how the media shape and are shaped by the other two components. The relationship between media and politics in un-democratic regimes will also be explored. Local and foreign political issues will be taken as examples for illustration.
courses.learning_outcomes:
(a) Enhancement of media and information literacy.

(b) Identification of the interactions between politicians and journalists.

(c) Comprehension and analysis of the key issues, concepts and debates in the field of politics and mass media studies.

(d) Application of the concepts, theories and knowledge in local and global context.

(e) Development of ability to think critically about the role of the media.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Week 1 Introduction: What are the relations of the Media and Politics?**

This lecture will outline the main themes and key issues which will be discussed in this course, the structure of tutorial session and assessment requirement.

* *A. Changes of Mass Media**

* *Week 2 The Hong Kong Media Landscape**

A free and independent media is crucial to democracy. The Basic Law protects the Press Freedom of Hong Kong. But how free is it and to what extent? Who owns our Media?

This lecture will talk about the ownership and regulation of Hong Kong’s media and discuss what kinds of relations that are developed between media owners, the government and society.

* *Week 3 Tabloidization of Mass Media**

Which newspaper you read everyday? (Or you don’t read newspaper!) What kind of news or which pages you read most frequently? Do you like ‘Li Bai Fong’ page of Appledaily?

Tabloidization reflects the change of news value in mass media. This lecture will talk about how and why hard news was replaced by soft news, in-depth news report was replaced by short article with eye-catching headline and soundbite.

* *B. Changes in Contemporary Politics**

* *Week 4 Americanization of Politics**

Americanization of political campaign happens in most democratic countries. Even in Hong Kong, live TV debate becomes a common practice during Elections. This lecture will talk about this transformation, the role of political advertising and political consultant in modern politics.

* *Week 5 Mass Media and Social Movement**

The relationship of mass media and social movement turns sour. It was reflected in the Anti-Express Rail Link Movement. Media were widely criticized by the activists as ignoring or underplaying the level of public concern.

This lecture will talk about the role of mass media in social movement and revolution in global context and investigate how it has changed since Vietnam War.

* *C. Interaction between Politics and Mass Media**

* *Week 6 Politicians and journalists**

Who is political journalist? Media and politician, who is more powerful? This lecture will talk about the complicated relations between journalists and news sources.

* *Week 7 Mid-term Exam**

* *Week 8 Politicians and mass media**

Everyday, there are numerous stories in newspapers quoting the unnamed “source” in different Hong Kong newspapers. Background briefing organized by government officials to announce or explain government polices behind the scene becomes more and more common.

This lecture will talk about how politicians manipulate the media. The communication strategy of UK New Labour government(1997-2010) will be studied as an example.

* *Week 9 Legislative Council and Mass Media**

Legislative Council meetings are often underplayed by local mass media. It is because the readers have no interest in the Legislators or the mass media themselves have no interested in them?

This lecture will talk about why Legislative Council meeting are often ignored by media. How parliament meetings are reported in the UK will be studied as an example as well.

* *Week 10 Public opinion**

Does public opinion exist? Is SAR government really that unpopular? This lecture will talk about the role of public in political communication process.

* *D. Cases Studies**

* *Week 11 The media and politics in China**

This lecture will introduce the media system, the mechanism of news control and how reporters and media such as *the Caijing* or *the Southern metropolitan* media group resist the news censorship in China.

* *Week 12 Al Jazeera phenomenon**

The Middle East News Channel, Al Jazeera TV generates a great impact in global politics. This lecture will briefly introduce how Al Jazeera transforms the global political landscape.

* *Week 13 Conclusion**

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Fundamentals of Government
courses.description:
(UGEC1512 is double-coded with GPAD1020.)

Introduction to the nature of political inquiry, the basic concepts in political science and the main features of the major types of political systems. Some emphasis is placed on the institutional aspects of government and administration, with concrete examples.
courses.learning_outcomes: Students are expected to acquire from this course general and fundamental knowledge about what is politics, how inevitable is it in our daily life, how power is organized and diffused around us, and how to think politically.
courses.course_syllabus:
Topics covered in the course:

What is politics

Nationalism and Birth of Modern States

Erection of Modern States in the Developed World

Erection of Modern States in the Developing World

Interest Groups, Political Parties and Electoral Systems

Legislatures, Executives and Bureaucracy

Constitution and Judiciary

Civil Society and Civil Disobedience

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Fundamentals of Public Administration
courses.description:
(UGEC1522 is double-coded with GPAD1030.)

This course introduces contemporary study and practice of public administration, and the characteristics and roles of public bureaucracy in various types of administrative systems. Students will also learn to analyze some issues such as codes of conduct for civil servants, budgetary politics and redress of administrative grievances.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken UGEC1732.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After taking this course, it is expected that students will have developed the following capabilities and skills:

* By the end of this introductory course students should be able to develop broad sweep knowledge of a range of approaches to the study of public administration and, to a certain degree, an in-depth knowledge of selected concepts and theories. These theories were selected because of their direct relevance for developing an understanding of modern government behaviour.
* Public administration is often considered in a managerial manner, as little more than control and straightforward analysis of efficiency and economy. This is not necessarily the case. People from different walks of life tend to have different expectations of government. This course is intended to foster a change in attitudes among students. After the course, students are expected to have the ability and tendency to think critically from multidisciplinary perspectives and to provide a critical appreciation of the strengths and weaknesses of any theoretical approach.
* Most important of all, by taking this course, students should have developed an understanding of the basic concepts and theories in public administration and should have gained the ability to apply them to the context of Hong Kong. Public administration, as an area of study, is very much a study based in American context. Therefore, it is important for students to reflect on the concepts and approaches covered in the course, and to consider how they can contribute to a better understanding of public administration in Hong Kong.
* The course is intended to reinforce communication skills on the part of students. Through the training in debates and discussions, students should have developed the ability to convey abstract ideas and complicated issues in a concrete and organized manner.

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Government of Hong Kong
courses.description:
(UGEC1885 is double-coded with GPAD1050.)

This is an introductory course in government and politics of Hong Kong. It examines the following major issues: political institutions and processes; the various theoretical approaches to understand Hong Kong politics; the Basic Law and the principle of “one country, two systems”; the performance of the SAR Government since the return of sovereignty ; political culture; political parties and elections; and public administration and public policy. Emphasis is placed on recent changes and development, and the prospects for democratization.
courses.learning_outcomes:
(a) grasp the basic operation, composition and function of the political institutions like Chief Executive, Executive Council, Legislature, District Council and electoral system in Hong Kong

(b) understand past political developments, have a basic understanding of the political change and evolution in Hong Kong such as decolonization and parties development , and the current political issues

(c) use their understanding of the political system and political history of Hong Kong, e.g. political parties and electoral system, to analyze the current affairs of Hong Kong and political issues of the day.
courses.course_syllabus:
The course wants the students to acquire the basic skills and knowledge after taking this course:

(1)a basic understanding of the major political institution in Hong Kong: their function, composition, operation and evolution in the past;

(2)a basic understanding of past political developments

(3)a basic understanding of current political issues and debates

(4)ability to use the above knowledge to understand and analyse the current issues around them, and day-to-day public affairs in Hong Kong society.

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Government and Politics of China
courses.description:
(UGEC1540 is double-coded with GPAD1070.)

This is an introductory course that surveys key issues in Chinese politics, including the historical background of the system, the roles of the party/state, the style of leadership, the forms of popular participation, and the distinctive features of Chinese politics. The focus of this course is political change.

Classic essays by Sun Yatsen, Lu Xun, Mao Zedong and others provide students with grounding in the key problems modern China has faced, as well as the solutions it has pioneered. From that foundation, we will move on to contemporary institutions and intellectual debates over inequality, human rights, and the future shape of political reform in China.

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Reading Chinese Culture and Politics through Movies
courses.description:
(UGEA1515 is double-coded with GPAD1073.)

Since its birth in the late-19th/early-20th century, Chinese movies have provided a very rich pool of images and stories, real or fictional, through which political messages could be read, interpreted, constructed, and re-constructed. As politics is a reflection of culture, a systematic analysis of these movies will provide students with a “softer” way to understand the cultural context which shapes Chinese politics. After an introduction of the relationship between culture, movies, and politics, the course will examine how Chinese political culture could be read through Chinese movies produced under different political settings (Republican, Maoist, and post-Maoist China).
courses.learning_outcomes:
* *Knowledge outcomes**

1. Basic understanding of the relationship between culture, movies, and politics.
2. Exposure to major Chinese cultural-political issues as reflected in Chinese movies.

* *Skill outcomes**

1. Ability to apply relevant concepts/theories to comprehend Chinese political and cultural issues.
2. Ability to think critically.
3. Ability to think creatively.
4. Ability to relate the academic pursuits to life experience.
5. Independent learning capacity and initiative in identifying problems.
6. Communication and interpersonal skills.
7. Writing skills to convey political and cultural concepts and analysis.

* *Value outcomes**

1. Sense of political and cultural awareness.
2. Sense of civic and cultural engagement.
courses.course_syllabus:
Introduction: Chinese culture, movies, and Chinese politics

Major approaches in film reading: classical Marxism; Frankfurt School; postmodern approach; postcolonial critique, etc.

Basic issues of Chinese politics and cultural politics: the inter-relationship between politics and culture in the Chinese context.

Chinese movies under different political settings: changes in the mode of production, themes, and discursive strategies of Chinese movies during Republican, Maoist, and post-Maoist eras.

Chinese political culture in first- to post-sixth-generation Chinese movies: examination of characteristics Chinese political culture as reflected in different generations of Chinese movies.

Diaspora movies in Hong Kong and Taiwan: cinematic reflections on the relation between Hong Kong and Taiwan with mainland China.

Chinese movies and politics under globalization: impact of globalization on the mode of production and discursive practice of Chinese movies.

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Critical Debates in Hong Kong
courses.description: The objective of this course is to apply concepts and theories in politics to understand some major debates about current affairs in Hong Kong. Through participating in these debates, students are encouraged to develop their critical thinking and reflections on the society they live in.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Since current debates in HK are expected to be incorporated into this course, the exact topics to be covered each year will then change accordingly. The following topics are very likely to be covered:

• How to obtain a balance between Conservation and Development?
• Is Democracy a Universal Value?
• Should the local Democratic Movement be more Strategic or stay firm in Principle?
• Is HK a M-shape Society? Is the local Middle Class shrinking and the Class Polarization deteriorating?
• What is the line between Erotic and Pornographic?
• What do we mean by Separation of the Church and State? Is it an issue for HK?
• Is Stability the highest priority in China’s Development?
• Do Globalization or State Intervention create Poverty?

After taking this course and through participating into these debates, students can grasp the essential nature of these controversies, the related arguments, and then learn more about the society they are living. Their critical think can be developed as a result. Students can also learn various basic concepts and theories in politics. More important, all these knowledge would be applied to the local context.

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Issues of Political Philosophy
courses.description:
(UGED1660 is double-coded with GPAD1095.)

This course will introduce students to key issues of political philosophy, including political authority and obligation, democracy, social justice, and freedom and right, through conceptual analysis and rational assessment of the thought of major political philosophers.
courses.learning_outcomes:
* *Aims**

◊To foster critical understanding of a range of fundamental issues and concepts of political philosophy.

◊To foster interest in the history of western political thought.

◊To foster the ability to analyze and assess opposing arguments in political philosophy.

◊To foster appreciation of the relevance of political philosophy to contemporary political and social issues.

◊To foster the ability to read classics of political philosophy.

* *Objectives**

After taking this course, students are expected to be able to: -

◊have general understanding of the major development of western political thought.

◊grasp the basic concepts of political philosophy.

◊comprehend and critically analyze complex philosophical arguments.

◊learn how to write academic essays.

◊construct sustained arguments about major political theories, and apply them to the analysis of public affairs.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction: What is Political Philosophy? (導論：何謂政治哲學？)

2. State and its Authority (國家及其權威)

3. Man as Political Animal: Aristotle’s Political Thought

作為政治的動物：亞里士多德的政治思想

4. Social Contract Theory I: Hobbes

社會契約論I：霍布士

5. Social Contract Theory II: John Locke & Rousseau

社會契約論II：洛克及盧梭

6. Social Contract Theory III: Rousseau

社會契約論III：盧梭

7. Democracy (民主) I

8. Democracy (民主) II

9. Liberty I: Concepts of Liberty (自由的概念)

10. Liberty II: Value of Liberty (自由的價值)

11. Justice (公正)

12. Right (權利)

13. Equality (平等)

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Selected Topics in Hong Kong Politics I
courses.description: This course provides an introductory analysis of important topics in the government and politics of Hong Kong. Examples of these topics are political structure and process, election and party system, public policy and administration, political development and democratization of Hong Kong. The focus of the course may vary according to the area of expertise of the instructor and the interests of the class.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Students who have taken GPAD1077 cannot take this course, unless teacher's consent has been obtained.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After successfully completing this course, students are expected to achieve the following learning outcomes:

Knowledge outcomes:
1. Students are expected to have a basic understanding of the theories and concepts about different electoral systems
2. Students are expected to be familiar with the major academic literatures on the field, especially the cases in Hong Kong
3. Students are expected to apply theories in the reality through case studies in Hong Kong

Skills outcomes:
1. Students are expected to compare and critically evaluate the differences between different electoral institutions
2. Students are expected to apply and integrate the concepts and theories of electoral studies, to analyze the situation in Hong Kong
3. In the tutorial, students are expected to develop their presentation skills
4. In the group project, students are expected to develop their analytical skills and interpersonal skills to work as a team

Value outcomes:
1. Students are expected to develop their interests in Hong Kong politics, especially the electoral systems and implications in a long run
2. Students are expected to think about the interactive effects of political institutions and one’s political behaviors, and develop their sense of commitment and responsibility in the society
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Aspects of Electoral Studies and an Overview of Electoral System
2. HK Electoral System: Features and Changes
3. Political Marketing: A Conceptual Framework
4. Election Engineering
5. Political Cleavages and Campaign Issues
6. Strategic Voting
7. Candidates and Leaders Selection
8. Local Level Elections in HK
9. Voting Behaviors of HK People
10. Basic Theories and Concepts of Parties and Party System
11. Parties and Party System in HK
12. Part Finance and Campaign Finance

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Politics of Culture
courses.description: This course aims to provide students with perspectives to understand culture, which has been considered as a contestable thing and a domain of politics in critical debate. As the course will convey, it is through the complicated cultural-political processes that the modern political world has been created. With special reference to local cases, this course will equip students with the concepts and analytical tools to approach the culture-politics phenomena in modern human society.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge outcomes:
1. Exposure to the major socio-political issues in everyday life culture.
2. Awareness of the socio-political context of culture in the globalizing Hong Kong and the whole world.
3. Basic understanding of the literatures, concepts and analytical approaches in this field.

Skill outcomes:
1. Ability to think critically.
2. Ability to think creatively.
3. Ability to relate the academic pursuits to life experience.
4. Ability to relate everyday cultural issues to social and political theories.
5. Independent learning capacity and initiative in identifying research topics.
6. Communication and interpersonal skills to work in a team.
7. Presentation skills to convey political concepts and analysis.

Value outcomes:
1. Awareness of the importance of politics in everyday life and studies.
2. The sense of self-discovery and self-expression.
3. The sense of responsibility and commitment in life.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. On politics and culture: Postmodernity and the cultural politics
2. Culture and the politics of difference
3. Cultural hegemony: commodity culture in capitalist society
4. Politics in everyday life: Domination and subversion in everyday life culture
5. Politicized culture: State and the construction of nationality
6. Defending privacy: Politicized culture and lives in private domain
7. Politics of museum: “Power plays at the exhibition”
8. Mapping: Struggling for representation
9. Contested space: Architecture and society in modern era
10. Reconstruction: State projects and societal resistance
courses.feedback_evaluation:
1. Course evaluation
2. Email and direct exchanges with students
3. Self-reflection

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Selected Topics in Chinese Politics I
courses.description: This course conducts introductory studies on some important topics in Chinese political development since 1949. The topics may include issues such as political campaigns, politics of economic reform, political reform and state-society relations. The focus of the course may vary according to the area of expertise of the instructor and the interests of the class.
courses.learning_outcomes:

Graduate should possess knowledge of political science, including the following:
1. Values and philosophies in political science and public policy
2. Understanding of the classics in political science
3. Understanding of the methodologies in political science

Skills Outcomes:
Graduate should acquire various competences, including the followings:
1. Ability to think critically
2. Ability to think creatively
3. Ability to think politically
4. Ability to think about political problems and issues comparatively
5. Ability to integrate different fields of knowledge in political science
6. Ability to relate the academic pursuits to life experience
7. Analytical skills for problem-solving
8. Communication and interpersonal skills to work in a team
9. Presentations skills to convey political concepts and analysis
10. Ability to relate the academic pursuits to life experience
11. Self-managed and independent learning capacity
12. Life-long learning capacity and engagement

Value Outcomes:
Graduates should have a positive attitude towards issues concerning political science and personal development, including the followings:
1. Appreciation of different aspects of knowledge in political science
2. Awareness of the importance of politics in daily life and studies

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Public Governance and Civil Service in Hong Kong
courses.description: During the years since the establishment of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region Government, public governance in Hong Kong has undergone many changes. This course will explain the historical background to the present public governance and its constitutional framework. It will focus on the major changes to various aspects of public administration and on the changing role of the civil service.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Understand the principle and practice of public governance and the role of the civil service in Hong Kong

2. Understand how the HKSAR Government functions in relation to other stakeholders such as the Central Government, the Legislature and the different sectors of society

3. Understand the working environment and personnel practice in Government

4. Develop critical thinking, politically-sensitive skills, communicating and presentation skills

5. Be better equipped to meet the future challenges of working and living in Hong Kong
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Explain the historical background to the present public governance and its constitutional framework
2. Focus on the major changes to various aspects of public administration
3. Focus on the changing role of the civil service

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Political Research Methodology
courses.description: The course covers theories of research, structure and process of analysis, modes of observation and data analysis, and basic statistics.

courses.learning_outcomes: Students are expected to have developed the capabilities to: 1) identify puzzles, develop research hypotheses, and design questions to test hypotheses; 2) collect survey data, enter data, and transform data; and 3) perform basic univariate, bivariate, and multivariate data analyses.

3. Variables and Their Measurements
4. Sampling: Theory and Method
5. Survey Design, Survey Implementation, Data Entry, and Data Transformation

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Introductory Comparative Politics
courses.description: A beginner's course to introduce students to the comparative study of government and politics around the world. Not a juxtaposition and comparison of political systems, but an analytical discussion of the holistic system as well as partial aspects of systems across countries. Topics may include classification of political systems; conceptualization of political functions and processes; working and performance of political institutions; role of political culture; systemic, institutional or cultural effects of public policies; issues of development and decay; and the national-international linkage. Students will be familiarized with the major contending approaches to theory construction in comparative politics.
courses.course_syllabus:
Part I: Introduction
1. Introduction to Comparative Politics
2. Video show: Manufacturing Consent, Part I Thought Control in a Democratic Society
• Charles Hauss, Comparative Politics, Chapter 1
• Gerardo L. Munck, “The Past and Present of Comparative Politics”
3. Classification and Comparison
• Charles Hauss, Comparative Politics, Chapter 2
• Arend Lijphart, “Comparative Politics and the Comparative Method”

Part II: Nation-States
4. Nation-States: Geography, Society, Economy & Globalization
• Charles Hauss, Comparative Politics, Chapters 3-4
• Seymour Martin Lipset, “Some Social Requisites of Democracy: Economic Development and Political Legitimacy”
5. Political Ideologies: Conservatism, Liberalism, and Socialism
• Gad Horowitz, “Notes on "Conservatism, Liberalism and Socialism in Canada"”
• Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy at http://plato.stanford.edu/contents.html

Part III: Citizens and Interest Mediation
6. Citizen Attitudes and Political Behavior
• Charles Hauss, Comparative Politics, Chapters 5-6
• Russell J. Dalton, “Citizen Attitudes and Political Behavior”
7. Pressure Groups and Political Parties
• Charles Hauss, Comparative Politics, Chapters 7-8
• Pippa Norris, “Making Democracies Work: Social Capital and Civic Engagement in 47 Societies”

Part IV: The Polity: Structures and Institutions
8. Executives and Legislatures
• Charles Hauss, Comparative Politics, Chapters 9
• Robert Elgie, “From Linz to Tsebelis: Three Waves of Presidential/Parliamentary Studies?”

9. Multi-level Government: National and Sub-national
• Charles Hauss, Comparative Politics, Chapters 10, 13
• Liesbet Hooghe and Gary Marksu, “Unraveling the Central State, but How? Types of Multi-level Governance”

10. Implementation: the Bureaucracy
• Charles Hauss, Comparative Politics, Chapters 11-12
• Steven J. Balla, “Administrative Procedures and Political Control of the Bureaucracy”

Part V: Political Changes
11. Social Movement & Revolution
• Steven M. Buechler, “New Social Movement Theories”
• John Foran, “Theories of Revolution Revisited: Toward a Fourth Generation?”
12. Democratic Transition
• Charles Hauss, Comparative Politics, Chapter 15-16
• Valerie Bunce, “Comparative Democratization: Big and Bounded Generalizations”
13. Conclusion

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: International Organizations
courses.description: This course is designed for undergraduate students who are either interested in social/political studies in general or international affairs in particular. As the course title states, the course introduces main categories of organizations that play important roles in today's world: 1) the United Nations; 2) regional inter-governmental organizations, e.g., the EU; 3) issue specific international regime and institution, e.g., WTO; 4) transnational non-governmental organizations, e.g., Greenpeace. When studying these organizations, particular attention will be paid to organizational history, structures, decision-making processes, and political effect. In addition, this course also leads students through the main theoretical frameworks developed by international relations scholars to explain the political relevance of international organizations.

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Understanding Human Rights
courses.description:
(UGED2700 is double-coded with GPAD2300.)

This course is to expose students to the field of human rights as a cross-disciplinary sub-discipline in law and political science. This course will assist students to better understand the debate concerning human rights both at home and abroad. The course will cover the international law and political issues in this field, the application of human rights in the domestic context and major problems in international human rights practice, including, for example, questions of gender discrimination, humanitarian intervention in domestic conflicts, poverty and human rights, and questions of universalism and cultural relativism.
courses.learning_outcomes:
The course would like to achieve the following outcomes:

1. To introduce the broad contours of human rights law and concepts and the goals and practices of the human rights movement

2. To engage the central debates that underlie the human rights movement and explore the implications of such debates in discrete areas of human rights law

3. To assist students in exploring their own interests in the field and in developing and achieving their own realistic goals for the study of human rights.
courses.course_syllabus:
I. Introduction-What is the Human Rights Movement About?

II. International Law-A Foundation for Human Rights

A. Customary Law

B. General Principles

C. Treaties

D. Nuremberg Trial-An Historical Foundation for Human Rights

E. Perspectives on International Law

F. State Sovereignty and Human Rights

G. Natural Rights and Human Rights

III. Civil and Political Rights

A. UN Foundations-Charter, Universal Declaration and ICCPR

B. Enforcement Under the ICCPR-A Prototype

1. Basic Powers and Functions

2. Comparison with Other Human Rights Treaties

C. State Duties Respecting Rights

D. The Public-Private Distinction

E. Human Rights Resolutions and Customary Practice

IV. Social and Economic Justice-Human Rights for the Poor?

A. Historical and Theoretical Context

B. Relating the Two Sets of Rights

C. Justiciability and Domestic Enforcement

D. International Monitoring

E. The Right to Development

V. Human Rights and Culture -Are Human Rights Just Western?

A. The Roots and Foundations of Human Rights

B. The Competing Claim for Human Responsibilities

C. Universalism Versus Cultural Relativism

The East Asian Perspective

D. Cultural Wars: Gender, Religion and Children

1. Inequality for Women

2. International Standards and the Women's Convention (CEDAW)

3. Gender and Culture

4. Religion and Human Rights

5. The Rights of Children

VI. The UN Human Rights System

A. Basic Concepts and Structures

B. The Historic UN Commission on HR-Special Procedures

C. The New UN Human Rights Council and High Commissioner

D. UN Security Council-Humanitarian Intervention and Sanctions

VII. Terrorism, Torture and Human Rights-New Rules for the Age of Terror?

A. Defining Terrorism-The Human Rights Connection

B. Regulating Emergencies and Derogation from Human Rights

C. Regulating Detention--Guantanamo

D. Torture-Is it Ever Justified?

VIII. International Criminal Justice and Human Rights

A. Universal Jurisdiction-Background

B. Adhoc International Criminal Tribunals

C. The International Criminal Court

D. Alternatives-Truth Commissions

IX. Regional Arrangements-Examples for Asia?

A. Regional Versus Global Approaches

B. The European System

C. The Inter-American System

X. State Protection of Human Rights and Civil Society

A. Civil Society and NGOs-Supporting Human Rights

B. Constitutionalism

C. The Hong Kong Basic Law

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: International Politics
courses.description:
(UGEC2610 is double-coded with GPAD2345.)

Examination of the actors, dynamics, strategies and rules of the changing international political system, and patterns of interaction among the powers.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken GLSD2301 or UGEC2352.
courses.learning_outcomes: This course aims to help students make sense of the confusing daily stream of headlines comingfrom around the world by providing the background and conceptual tools students need to understand contemporary international relations. The course is designed to give an overview of the breadth and scope of the wide-ranging field of international relations. Along the way, students will get a taste of topics on international security, international political economy, international law, American foreign policy, and so on. The last part of the course will study some of the most important issues currently on the international agenda: terrorism, friction along civilizational fault-lines, current debates in American foreign policy, and the rise of China.

8. International Law and International Government

9. What is International Political Economy?

10. Independence and Globalization

11. Clash of Civilizations

12. The Uses of American Power

13. Emerging Great Power Rivalries: The Rise of China

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Contemporary Political Philosophy
courses.description: This course will introduce and examine major theories of contemporary Anglo-American political philosophy. Topics include utilitarianism, liberalism, libertarianism, Marxism, communitarianism, feminism and global justice.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge Outcomes:
1. Understanding important issues central to contemporary analytical political philosophy.
2. Ability to critically evaluate different political theories.
3. Knowing how to apply concepts and theories to analyze political systems and current affairs.
4. Having a broad understanding of the nature of politics, and the relevance of normative political theory to political science.
5. Exposure to the political thought of major political philosophers.
6. Understanding the methodologies and approaches in political theory.

Skill Outcomes:
1. Ability to think critically and creatively.
2. Ability to write rigorous philosophical essays.
3. Ability to apply what you have learnt to analyze social affairs and political issues.
4. Ability to present philosophical arguments systematically and clearly.
5. Communication and interpersonal skills to work as a team for group project.
6. Capacity to continue learning and researching on related issues independently.
7. Analytical skills for problem solving.

Value Outcomes:
1. Cultivating intellectual interest in thinking about human values and the meaning of life.
2. Ability to develop the sense of own judgments and values.
3. Awareness of the importance of philosophical thinking in daily life.
4. Reflecting on the idea of university and responsibility of intellectuals through philosophical enquiry.

1. Introduction
2. Utilitarianism (效益主義)

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: The Idea of Freedom
courses.description: Freedom is widely held to be a primary political value. But what is freedom? Why is it valuable? And what kinds of institutions are required to protect it? These are the questions addressed in this course. It will be divided into four parts. The first part examines the concept of freedom and its history. The second part considers the value of freedom and its justification. The third part investigates the relationship between freedom and other political values. The last part examines the role of freedom in different political theories.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken GPAD2146.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge Outcomes:
1. Understanding basic concepts of freedom.
2. Exposure to political thought of major political philosophers.
3. Establish foundation for further study of political philosophy.

Skill Outcomes:
1. Develop ability to think philosophically.
2. Learn how to read original texts and write academic essays.
3. Ability to apply political theories to analyze social affairs and political issues.
4. Learn to present arguments systematically in the tutorials.
5. Communication and interpersonal skills to work as a team for group project.
6. Develop ability to conduct independent research.

Value Outcomes:
1. Cultivate intellectual interest in philosophical enquiry.
2. Take human values seriously.
3. Awareness of the importance of leading an examined life.

1. Examine the concept of freedom and its history

3. Investigate the relationship between freedom and other political values
4. Examine the role of freedom in different political theories

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Governance of European Union
courses.description: The course introduces students to the governance of the European Union. It will cover the extensive scholarly debates which have accompanied the development of the European Union. Issues to be covered include: (1) the historical background to the formation of the European Union; (2) the early institutional mechanisms; (3) the record of the organization up to the end of the cold war in 1989; (4) the latest period of institution building (the European Union and the Constitution); and (5) the European Union’s present domestic organization and place in the global system.

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Politics of Space
courses.description: This is a trans-disciplinary course that aims at activating students' sensitivities in the dimension of space in understanding politics. Fundamental concepts and approaches developed in the field of politics of space will be introduced. The content includes also discussion of the following topics of interest: heritage preservation, contested public space, state project and societal resistance, consumerist space, etc. Substantive local and foreign cases will be used to demonstrate the complicated politico-spatial processes in society which are considered to be political, ideological and full of contradictions.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge outcomes:
1. Exposure to the major socio-political spatial issues in everyday life.
2. Awareness of the socio-political context of city space in the globalizing Hong Kong and the whole world.
3. Basic understanding of the literatures, concepts and analytical approaches in this field.

Skill outcomes:
1. Ability to think critically.
2. Ability to think creatively.
3. Ability to relate the academic pursuits to life experience.
4. Ability to relate everyday spatial issues to social and political theories.
5. Independent learning capacity and initiative in identifying research topics.
6. Communication and interpersonal skills to work in a team.
7. Presentation skills to convey political concepts and analysis.

Value outcomes:
1. Awareness of the importance of politics in everyday life and studies.
2. The sense of self-discovery and self-expression.
3. The sense of responsibility and commitment in life.
courses.course_syllabus:
Theoretical perspectives
1. Introduction: Space in the studies of politics
2. Mode of production and the production of space
3. Disciplinary regime and construction of space in modern time
4. Poststructuralist conception pf space
5. Spatial imaginations in the political classics
6. Space and “the political”

Cases
7. Heritage preservation: Development, politics of “disappearance” and postcolonial condition
8. Contested public space: Domination and subversion
9. Reconstruction: State projects and local resistance
10. Consumerist space: Globalization and resistance in everyday life
11. Marketing city: Museum and cultural projects

courses.description: This course aims to introduce students to the study and major issues in international political economy. It will begin with the major theories in international political economy. The discussion will then move to the important issues in international economy: trade, international monetary and financial policy, migration, globalization and its consequences, and development.

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Seminar in Political Theory
courses.description: This is a seminar course for senior undergraduates and graduate students, with in-depth studies and evaluation of contemporary debate of political theories. Topics may include rational choice theory, theories of justice, egalitarianism, post-modernism, global justice, and the latest development in the discipline.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students are expected to acquire the following learning outcomes.
Knowledge Outcomes
1. Understanding important issues central to contemporary analytical political philosophy.
2. Ability to critically evaluate different political theories.
3. Knowing how to apply concepts and theories to analyze political systems and current affairs.
4. Having a broad understanding of the nature of politics, and the relevance of normative political theory to political science.
5. Exposure to the political thought of major political philosophers.
6. Understanding the methodologies and approaches in political theory.
Skill Outcomes
1. Ability to think critically and creatively.
2. Ability to write rigorous philosophical essays.
3. Ability to apply what you have learnt to analyze social affairs and political issues.
4. Ability to present philosophical arguments systematically and clearly.
5. Communication and interpersonal skills to work as a team for group project.
6. Capacity to continue learning and researching on related issues independently.
7. Analytical skills for problem solving.
Value Outcomes
1. Cultivating intellectual interest in thinking about human values and the meaning of life.
2. Ability to develop the sense of own judgments and values.
3. Awareness of the importance of philosophical thinking in daily life.
4. Reflecting on the idea of university and responsibility of intellectuals through philosophical enquiry.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction: The Development of Contemporary Political Philosophy

2& 3 Liberalism （自由主義）

4 & 5. Communitarianism (社群主義)

6 & 7 Multiculturalism (文化多元主義)

8& 9 Republicanism (共和主義)

10&11 Politics of Difference(差異政治）

12 & 13 Political Liberalism (政治自由主義)

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Modern Chinese Political Thought
courses.description: This course will consider the major developments of Chinese political though from late Qing to the present day. It will examine from philosophical and historical perspective show Chinese intellectuals responded to the challenge of modernization. The topics to be discussed include the tension between Chinese tradition and modernity, conservatism, neo-Confucianism, socialism, liberalism, nationalism and the debate between the new-left and libertarianism.
courses.learning_outcomes:
知識成效 (Knowledge Skills)：
1. 對現代中國政治思想史有一概括性認識。
2. 對不同時期不同學派的政治主張有基本了解。
3. 對中國現代政治思想的一些核心概念，有基本的把握。
4. 能運用不同的理論分析框架，詮釋中國現代政治思潮。
5. 對思想史的比較研究有基本認識。
6. 對政治思想史的研究方法有基本掌握。

學習技巧　（Learning Skills）：
1. 閱讀及理解文獻的能力。
2. 設定研究問題及作獨立研究的能力。
3. 撰寫中文學術論文的能力。
4. 與他人合作及共同研究的能力。

價值培養（Values Outcomes）：
1. 加深對中國文化及傳統的認識，並嘗試反思個人及民族身份問題。
2. 反思知識分子在現代中國扮演的角色問題。
3. 培養學生的社會關懷。
4. 增加學生對中國問題的關心。
courses.course_syllabus:
一 導論
二 中國現代思想發展概觀
三 從傳統到現代
四 西方政治思想的傳入：嚴復思想研究
五/六 五四運動及其詮釋
七 儒家／烏托邦／馬克思主義
八 革命vs.改良/激進vs.保守
九 新儒家
十 民族主義的理念
十一 中國民族主義的發展
十二 八十年代：新啟蒙運動
十三 新左派 vs. 自由主義

courses.description: This course aims to provide students with on-site learning experience for their integration of the theories and concepts in political science and public administration they learn in regular classes. Students will work at institutions recognized by the Department for no less than 200 hours. Students will be asked to attend orientation and sharing sessions before and after their placement.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge outcomes:

* To develop deeper understanding of political science and public administration within institutional and industry settings
* To further understand the theories and concepts of political science and public administration when they are applied within institutional and industry settings

Skill outcomes:

* To develop problem solving skills in institutional and industry settings
* To learn the specific technical skills in institutional and industry settings
* To acquire critical skills through observation and direct handling of problems in various institutional and industry settings

Value outcomes:

* To be more aware of the complexity of society
* To be more aware of the importance as well as difficulties in upholding certain values

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Qualitative Methods of Political Research
courses.description: This course serves to introduce postgraduate students to the fundamental principles of scientific inquiry and major qualitative research methods. Topics to be covered include: conceptual foundation of research in social science, design and structure of political research, and methods for collecting and analyzing qualitative data. Undergraduate students taking this course should first obtain teacher's consent.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Government and Public Administration
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the semester, students are expected to have acquired the
capabilities to: 1) understand the fundamental principles of social science
research; 2) design rigorous political research with qualitative methods; 3) and
critically evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of political research design.

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Advanced Social Science Research Methods
courses.description: This course seeks to help post-graduate students engage with advanced quantitative issues in social science research. The course is organized around modules based on (hard) methodological challenges that social science researchers must confront at the stage of research design or data analysis, or both. The course is conceived as the last course in quantitative training to be offered for second year postgraduate students who are in the process of crafting their own thesis proposal. This course follows GPAD5055 or equivalent. Students who have not passed GPAD5055 need the instructor’s permission to enroll.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Government and Public Administration
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the semester, students are expected to have improved their knowledge and ability to: 1) Devise an analytic strategy to estimate quantitative models appropriate to answer a specific research questions and clearly and precisely report their findings in a transparent and fully replicable manner (including the creation of replication code); 2) perform statistical analyses and perform the necessary robustness checks expected of scholars who submit to professional journals in applied social science; 3) properly interpret the results of empirical analyses and clearly articulate the links between theory and evidence.

GRADE DESCRIPTORS
• A Corresponds to professional work. The quality of the work is nearly on par with what is required for submission in a journal
in the discipline.
• B Demonstrates mastery the basics concepts, but the work is not at the level required for submission to professional
journals.
• C Demonstrates acceptable mastery the basics concepts, but there are mistakes in data analysis and/or interpretation.
• D Poor mastery of the basic concepts
• F No understanding of the basic concepts
courses.course_syllabus:
This course relies on a number of software packages, primarily STATA, though selected use of R, QGIS, SAGA and OpenGEODA (inter alia) will also be necessary for some of the modules.

5907. 2012.00602.x

• Gary King, James Honaker, Anne Joseph, and Kenneth Scheve. 2001. “Analyzing Incomplete Political Science Data: An
Alternative Algorithm for Multiple Imputation.” American Political Science Review, 95, Pp. 49–69.Abstract Article
• Andrew Gelman, Gary King, and Chuanhai Liu. 1999. “Not Asked and Not Answered: Multiple Imputation for Multiple
Surveys.” Journal of the American Statistical Association, 93, Pp. 846–857.Abstract Article
• Explore: https://gking.harvard.edu/amelia and install it on your computer (including R if necessary)
• Further recommended readings
• James Honaker and Gary King. 2010. “What to do About Missing Values in Time Series Cross-Section Data.” American
Journal of Political Science, 54, 3, Pp. 561-581. Article
• Matthew Blackwell, James Honaker, and Gary King. 2017. “A Unified Approach to Measurement Error and Missing Data:
Overview and Applications.” Sociological Methods and Research, 46, 3, Pp. 303-341. Article

WEEK 4 Modelling Selection Bias
• Predicting online political participation: The importance of selection bias and selective exposure in the online setting.
• Felix Elwert and Christopher Winship. 2014. Endogenous Selection Bias: The Problem of Conditioning on a Collider
Variable. Annual Review of Sociology Vol. 40:31-53.
• Kim et. al. Politics, Institutions, and Trade. 2016

WEEK 5 Dealing with Endogeneity
• Neumayer, E., and T. Plümper. Robustness Tests for the Analysis of Observational Data, Chapter 11.
• Gelman & Hill Chapters 9 & 10.
• Thad Dunning. 2012. Natural Experiments in the Social Sciences: A Design-Based Approach. Cambridge University Press
(Part 1)
• Bear F. Braumoeller , Giampiero Marra , Rosalba Radice and Aisha E. Bradshaw. Flexible Causal Inference for Political
Science. Political Analysis.

Recommended
• Christopher H. Achen. Why lagged dependent variables can suppress the explanatory power of other independent variables

WEEK 6 Multi-level analysis (this week and next are closely related)
• Neumayer, E., and T. Plümper. Robustness Tests for the Analysis of Observational Data, Chapter 12.
• Gelman & Hill Chapters 11-15
• Bradford S. Jones. 2007, MULTILEVEL MODELS (manuscript)
• Recommended: Raudenbush & Bryk

WEEK 7 Advanced regression analysis: fixed or random effects?
• Neumayer, E., and T. Plümper. Robustness Tests for the Analysis of Observational Data, Ch13:Effect Dynamics.
• Paul Clarke, Claire Crawford, Fiona Steele & Anna Vignoles (2013) Revisiting fixed- and random-effects models: some
considerations for policy-relevant education research, Education Economics,23:3, 259-277, DOI:
10. 1080/09645292.2013.855705
• Andrew Bell and Kelvyn Jones (2014) Explaining Fixed Effects: Random Effects Modeling of Time-Series Cross-Sectional
and Panel Data. Political Science Research and Methods / FirstView Article / May 2014, pp 1 - 21 DOI:
10. 1017/psrm.2014.7, Published online: 01 May 2014.
• Larry Bartels. 2016. BEYOND “FIXED VERSUS RANDOM EFFECTS”: A FRAMEWORK FOR IMPROVING SUBSTANTIVE
AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS OF PANEL, TIME-SERIES CROSS-SECTIONAL, AND MULTILEVEL DATA.

WEEK 7 Advanced regression analysis: Sampling weights, post-stratification and design effects
• Neumayer, E., and T. Plümper. Robustness Tests for the Analysis of Observational Data, Ch 7.Population and Sample
• Sharon L. Lohr. 2009. Sampling: Design and Analysis. Second edition. (Selections)
• Andrew G. Walder, Bobai Li and Donald J. Treiman. 2000. Politics and Life Chances in a State Socialist Regime: Dual
Career Paths into the Urban Chinese Elite, 1949 to 1996. American Sociological Review, Vol. 65, No. 2 (Apr., 2000), pp.
191-209.

WEEK 8 Advanced regression analysis: switching models and regression discontinuity analysis
• Brandon de la Cuesta & Kosuke Imai.2016. Misunderstandings About the Regression Discontinuity Design in the Study of
Close Elections. Annual Review of Political Science. 2016. 19:375–96.
• Avraham Ebenstein et. al. 2017. New evidence on the impact of sustained exposure to air pollution on life expectancy from

Recommended
• Guido W. Imbens & Thomas Lemieux. 2008. Regression discontinuity designs: A guide to practice. Journal of Econometrics,
Vol 142, Issue 2, February 2008, pp. 615-635.

WEEK 10 Designing and analyzing experiments
• Thad Dunning. 2012. Natural Experiments in the Social Sciences: A Design-Based Approach. Cambridge University Press
(Part 2 and Part 3)
• Avidit Acharya, Matthew Blackwell, and Maya Sen. 2016. Analyzing Causal Mechanisms in Survey Experiments
• Mullinix, Kevin J., Leeper, Thomas J., Druckman, James N. and Freese, Jeremy (2015) The generalizability of survey
experiments. Journal of Experimental Political Science, 2 (2). pp. 109- 138. ISSN 2052-2630 DOI: 10.1017/XPS.2015.19
• KING, G., MURRAY, C., SALOMON, J., & TANDON, A. (2004). Enhancing the Validity and Cross-Cultural Comparability of
Measurement in Survey Research. American Political Science Review, 98(1), 191-207. doi:10.1017/S000305540400108X

WEEK 11 Introduction to Spatial econometrics
• Neumayer, E., and T. Plümper. Robustness Tests for the Analysis of Observational Data, Ch14: Spatial Correlation and

• Anselin, L. 1995. “Local Indicators of Spatial Association – LISA.” Geographical Analysis 27: 93-115.

Further Readings

WEEK 12 Is big data such a big deal? Pixels and People: Big data, remote sensing and GIS
• Bonica, Adam (2014). “Mapping the Ideological Marketplace”. American Journal of Political Science 58 (2): pp. 367–386.

• Kieran Healy. 2018. Data Visualization A practical introduction. Forthcoming, Princeton University Press. (selections)

courses.subject: GPAD
courses.title: Contemporary Political Science
courses.description: The objective of this course is to acquaint students with the state of the art in political science. The course will analyse recent developments of the discipline pertaining to its scope, methodology, theory and approach. Particular attention will be given to areas where debates and controversies have contributed to the development of knowledge in the field. The ethos, concerns and research concentration of the discipline will also be discussed. Undergraduate students taking this course should first obtain teacher's consent.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Government and Public Administration
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. The students should understand the intellectual evolution, key issues and selected theories in contemporary political science.
2. The students should get familiar with major methodological approaches and analytical tools in political studies.
3. The students should develop the ability to critically evaluate existing political science literature and articulate their own scholarly views.
courses.course_syllabus:
Introduction: Methodological Foundation

Gary King, Robert O. Keohane, and Sidney Verba. 1994. Designing Social Inquiry: Scientific Inference in Qualitative Research. Princeton University Press, Chapters 1, 3, 4
Henry E. Brady and David Collier, eds. 2010. Rethinking Social Inquiry: Diverse Tools, Shared Standards. 2nd Edition. Rowman & Littlefield Publishers, Chapters 4, 6

Modernity and Modernization

Samuel.P. Huntington. 1968. Political Order in Changing Societies. Henry L. Stimson lectures, Yale University. Yale University Press, pp 1–92

Robert A. Dahl. [1961] 2005. Who Governs? Yale University Press, Book I

Seymour Martin Lipset. 1959. “Some Social Requisites of Democracy: Economic Development and Political Legitimacy”. American Political Science Review 53:69–105

Adam Przeworski and Fernando Limongi. 1997. “Modernization: Theories and Facts”. World Politics 49 (2): 155–183

Democracy and Democratization

Barrington Moore. 1966. Social Origins of Dictatorship and Democracy. Boston, MA: Beacon Press, pp 3–29, 413–484

Carles Boix. 2003. Democracy and Redistribution. Cambridge University Press, pp 19–59

Daron Acemoglu and James A. Robinson. 2006. Economic Origins of Democracy and Dictatorship. Cambridge University Press, pp 1–47

Ben Ansell and David Samuels. 2010. “Inequality and Democratization: A Contractarian Approach”. Comparative Political Studies 43 (12): 1543–1574

Authoritarianism

Juan Linz. 1964. “An Authoritarian Regime: Spain”. In Cleavages, ideologies and Party Systems, ed. by Erik Allardt and Yrjö Littunen, 291–341. The Academic Bookstore

Barbara Geddes. 2003. Paradigms and Sand Castles: Theory Building and Research Design in Comparative Politics. University of Michigan Press, pp 47–85

Steven Levitsky and Lucan Way. 2002. “The Rise of Competitive Authoritarianism”. Journal of Democracy 13 (2): 51–65

Milan Svolik. 2012. The Politics of Authoritarian Rule. Cambridge University Press, Chapters 1–3

Institutions

Douglass C. North. 1990. Institutions, Institutional Change and Economic Performance. Cambridge University Press, pp 1–69, 83-106

Peter A. Hall and Rosemary C. R. Taylor. 1996. “Political Science and the Three New Institutionalisms\*”. Political Studies 44 (5): 936–957

Paul Pierson. 2000. “Increasing Returns, Path Dependence, and the Study of Politics”. The American Political Science Review 94 (2): 251–267

Wolfgang Streeck and Kathleen A. Thelen. 2005. “Introduction: Institutional Change in Advanced Political Economies”. In Beyond Continuity: Institutional Change in Advanced Political Economies. Oxford University Press

Gretchen Helmke and Steven Levitsky. 2004. “Informal Institutions and Comparative Politics: A Research Agenda”. Perspectives on Politics 2 (4): 725–740

Anna Grzymala-Busse. 2010. “The Best Laid Plans: The Impact of Informal Rules on Formal Institutions in Transitional Regimes”. Studies in Comparative International Development 45 (3): 311–333

State and State-building

Mancur Olson. 1993. “Dictatorship, Democracy, and Development”. The American Political Science Review 87 (3): 567–576

Charles Tilly. 1985. “War Making and State Making as Organized Crime”. In Bringing the State Back In, ed. by Peter B. Evans, Dietrich Rueschemeyer, and Theda Skocpol, 169–191. Cambridge University Press

Raúl Sánchez de la Sierra. 2020. “On the Origins of the State: Stationary Bandits and Taxation in Eastern Congo”. Journal of Political Economy 128 (1): 32–74

Bureaucracy

Peter B. Evans. 1995. Embedded Autonomy: States and Industrial Transformation. Vol. 25. Cambridge University Press, pp 1–72

Bo Rothstein. 2014. “The Chinese Paradox of High Growth and Low Quality of Government: The Cadre Organization Meets Max Weber”. Governance: 533–548

Lloyd I. Rudolph and Susanne Hoeber Rudolph. 1979. “Authority and Power in Bureaucratic and Patrimonial Administration: A Revisionist Interpretation of Weber on Bureaucracy”. World Politics 31 (2): 195–227

Margaret Boittin, Greg Distelhorst, and Francis Fukuyama. 2016. Reassessing the Quality of Government in China. Working Paper. <https://ssrn.com/abstract=2875244>

Political Economy of Development

Douglass C. North and Barry R. Weingast. 1989. “Constitutions and Commitment: The Evolution of Institutions Governing Public Choice in Seventeenth-Century England”. The
Journal of Economic History 49, no. 4 (): 803–832

Daron Acemoglu, Simon Johnson, and James A Robinson. 2001. “The Colonial Origins of Comparative Development: An Empirical Investigation”. American Economic Review 91 (5): 1369–1401, also see David Y. Albouy. 2012. “The Colonial Origins of Comparative Development: An Empirical Investigation: Comment”. American Economic Review 102 (6): 3059–3076

Nathan Nunn and Diego Puga. 2012. “Ruggedness: The Blessing of Bad Geography in Africa”. Review of Economics and Statistics 94 (1): 20–36

Benjamin F. Jones and Benjamin A. Olken. 2005. “Do Leaders Matter? National Leadership and Growth Since World War II”. The Quarterly Journal of Economics 120 (3): 835–864

Election and Accountability

Morris P. Fiorina. 1978. “Economic Retrospective Voting in American National Elections: A Micro-Analysis”. American Journal of Political Science 22 (2): 426–443

James D. Fearon. 1999. “Electoral Accountability and the Control of Politicians: Selecting Good Types Versus Sanctioning Poor Performance”. Democracy, Accountability, and Representation 55:61

Andrew J. Healy, Neil Malhotra, and Cecilia Hyunjung Mo. 2010. “Irrelevant events affect voters’ evaluations of government performance”. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 107 (29): 12804–12809; Also see Anthony Fowler and B. Pablo Montagnes. 2015. “College football, elections, and false-positive results in observational research”. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 112 (45): 13800–13804

Scott Ashworth and Ethan Bueno De Mesquita. 2014. “Is Voter Competence Good for Voters?: Information, Rationality, and Democratic Performance”. American Political Science Review 108 (3): 565–587

Civil Society

Robert D. Putnam. 1994. Making Democracy Work: Civic Traditions in Modern Italy. Princeton paperbacks. Princeton University Press, Chapters 1, 4, 6

Sheri Berman. 1997. “Civil Society and the Collapse of the Weimar Republic”. World Politics 49 (3): 401–429

Lily L. Tsai. 2007. “Solidary Groups, Informal Accountability, and Local Public Goods Provision in Rural China”. American Political Science Review 101 (02): 355–372

Daniel C. Mattingly. 2016. “Elite Capture”. World Politics 68 (03): 383–412

Social Movements and Contentious Politics

Sidney Tarrow. [1994] 2011. Power in Movement: Social Movements, Collective Action and Politics. Cambridge University Press, Introduction and Chapters 1, 8

Jim Scott. 1986. “Everyday forms of peasant resistance”. The Journal of Peasant Studies 13 (2): 5–35

Dingxin Zhao. 1998. “Ecologies of Social Movements: Student Mobilization during the 1989 Prodemocracy Movement in Beijing”. American Journal of Sociology 103 (6): 1493–1529
